metadata.summary: Zero-runtime CSS-in-JS extracts styles at build time (vanilla-extract, Panda, StyleX). SSR extraction prevents FOUC for runtime libraries.

content.title: CSS-in-JS Advanced
content.summary: Zero-runtime CSS-in-JS (vanilla-extract, Panda CSS, StyleX) extracts styles at build time, producing plain CSS with no runtime serialization cost. Use recipe() for typed variants, createVar() for CSS variable contract, and Sprinkles for a constrained atomic utility layer. SSR style extraction for runtime libraries (styled-components, Emotion) requires a server-side registry and a FOUC Playwright assertion.

why.p: Runtime CSS-in-JS dominates Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) in large applications because the browser cannot paint until JavaScript serializes and injects styles. Migrating to zero-runtime removes this bottleneck and makes components compatible with React Server Components.

Sprinkles and Panda recipes encode the design token contract directly in TypeScript, making invalid token combinations a compile error instead of a runtime footgun. This prevents entire classes of styling bugs and improves developer experience with autocomplete.

list.item: Migrating styled-components / Emotion codebase to a zero-runtime solution (vanilla-extract, Linaria, Panda CSS, StyleX) for Core Web Vitals or React Server Components.
Building a typed atomic CSS layer (Sprinkles / Panda recipes) when Tailwind doesn't fit (e.g., heavy theming, multi-brand white-label).
Setting up SSR style extraction for a styled-components / Emotion app on Next.js Pages Router or Remix.
Optimizing a runtime CSS-in-JS app whose Largest Contentful Paint is dominated by serialized CSS injection.
New apps where Tailwind + CSS variables solves the problem — runtime CSS-in-JS adds bundle and hydration cost without theming gains.
React Server Components-only trees: styled-components and Emotion runtime require a 'use client' boundary; pick vanilla-extract, Panda, or StyleX instead.
Component libraries published to npm — runtime CSS-in-JS forces the consumer's bundler to ship the runtime; ship plain CSS or zero-runtime output.

section.name: vanilla-extract-core
section.title: vanilla-extract Core: recipe() and createVar()
section.p: Use createVar() to declare CSS custom properties and recipe() to combine base styles with typed variant maps. Always set defaultVariants. createVar() produces a named CSS variable with TypeScript type; omitting defaultVariants causes runtime errors when the recipe receives undefined for a required variant.

example.title: vanilla-extract recipe with createVar

rule.statement: Always set defaultVariants in a recipe() definition. When a consumer calls button({ color: undefined }), vanilla-extract must have a fallback variant.
rule.rationale: Omitting defaultVariants causes runtime errors on undefined variant slots. The recipe type cannot be narrowed at call time if variants are optional.

section.name: vanilla-extract-sprinkles
section.title: Sprinkles and Budget Rules
section.p: Treat Sprinkles properties like a public API. Audit generated CSS size in CI. Every added property compiles to N classnames where N = number of values × number of conditions.

rule.statement: Keep Sprinkles properties narrowly scoped. Every added property expands CSS by multiplying its value count by the condition count. Over-broad arrays explode CSS by 5-10x.
rule.rationale: The resulting stylesheet causes measurable parse-time LCP regressions on mobile. A 500kb stylesheet can delay first paint by 200-500ms on 3G networks.

section.p: When defining Sprinkles properties, constrain to tokens that actually vary (colors, sizes, spacing) and avoid explosion by keeping value arrays small. Monitor CSS output in CI builds.

section.name: vanilla-extract-build-sandbox
section.title: Build Sandbox Constraints
section.p: Keep .css.ts files dependency-free of app code. Only import other .css.ts files, types, and literal constants. Never reference window, process.env, or dynamic imports.

rule.statement: vanilla-extract runs .css.ts files in a restricted Node sandbox at build time. Do not import app-level modules that reference browser globals or environment checks.
rule.rationale: Importing app code that uses window or process.env crashes the build with cryptic "cannot find module" errors. The sandbox has no browser APIs and cannot evaluate async imports.

example.title: Broken build: importing app code
Correct: literal tokens only

section.name: ssr-styled-components-registry
section.title: Server-Side Style Extraction: styled-components
section.p: For Next.js App Router with styled-components, wrap the tree in a ServerStyleSheet registry component with "use client" and compiler.styledComponents enabled in next.config.js.

rule.statement: styled-components v6 without the registry produces hydration mismatches because CSS is injected client-side after the server-rendered HTML is painted.
rule.rationale: The browser paints unstyled HTML (Flash of Unstyled Content) before JS runs and applies styles. The registry injects style tags into the server response, preventing FOUC.

example.title: styled-components registry for App Router

section.p: Also ensure next.config.js has: compiler: { styledComponents: true }

section.name: fouc-assertion
section.title: FOUC Prevention: Playwright Assertion
section.p: Write a Playwright test that fetches the page HTML and asserts a style tag with data-styled or equivalent attribute is present in the initial response body.

rule.statement: FOUC is only visible on slow networks; it cannot be caught in development but fails user perception on first paint in production. Always write a Playwright test that confirms styles are in the initial HTML.
rule.rationale: Developers with fast networks and cached assets never see FOUC locally. It only appears on first visit over 3G. Tests catch this before production.

example.title: Playwright FOUC test

section.name: emotion-ssr
section.title: Server-Side Style Extraction: Emotion
section.p: Emotion SSR requires a CacheProvider and extractCritical for Pages Router, or a client-side CacheProvider wrapper for App Router.

rule.statement: Emotion runtime requires a client context; without the CacheProvider wrapper in App Router, the css prop silently degrades to a plain style attribute and FOUC occurs.
rule.rationale: Emotion's CSS-in-JS machinery requires a React context to collect styles. App Router's default server behavior cannot use that context; the wrapper bridges it.

antipattern.description: Defining styled.div or recipe() calls inside a React component render body.
antipattern.reason: Creates a new component class or stylesheet on every render, causing style cache misses and performance regressions. Move styled components and recipes to module scope; pass dynamic values via CSS variables or transient props.

antipattern.description: Using vars: { color: '#3b82f6' } in vanilla-extract instead of createVar() + assignVars.
antipattern.reason: Inline var assignment bypasses the typed contract; switching themes requires touching every .css.ts file instead of reassigning variables at the theme boundary. Use createVar() to decouple style definitions from runtime theme values.

antipattern.description: Omitting defaultVariants in a recipe() definition.
antipattern.reason: When a consumer calls button({ color: undefined }), vanilla-extract throws at runtime because no fallback exists for the undefined variant slot. Always provide sensible defaults.

antipattern.description: Adding Emotion to Next.js App Router without the 'use client' CacheProvider wrapper.
antipattern.reason: Emotion runtime requires a client context; without the wrapper, the css prop silently degrades to a plain style attribute and FOUC occurs on first paint. Styling appears to work in development but fails in production or on cold cache.

antipattern.description: Importing app-level modules (environment checks, API clients) from .css.ts files.
antipattern.reason: vanilla-extract runs .css.ts in a Node build sandbox; browser globals and async imports are unavailable and crash the build with cryptic "cannot find module" errors. Keep .css.ts pure and dependency-free of runtime code.

antipattern.description: Memoizing css template literal calls instead of lifting styled components to module scope.
antipattern.reason: Memoization reduces re-computation but the component class is still created inside the component function; only module-scope definitions produce stable class names and prevent style churn.

antipattern.description: Copying RecipeVariants<typeof button> but forgetting the matching defaultVariants.
antipattern.reason: The recipe type is inferred from variants but requires a defaultVariants object that matches the variant map. Agents often copy types without realizing the runtime dependency on defaults.

section.name: panda-css
section.title: Panda CSS Alternative
section.p: Panda CSS provides a zero-runtime alternative built on CSS variables and atomic CSS generation. Use panda.config.ts to define tokens, recipes, and utilities. Panda generates utility classes like vanilla-extract recipes with zero runtime overhead.
Key advantage: Panda includes a Tailwind-like utility layer for quick styling without hand-rolling Sprinkles. Good for teams migrating from Tailwind but needing stronger theming control.

section.name: stylex
section.title: StyleX (Meta)
section.p: StyleX is a compile-time CSS-in-JS framework from Meta (Instagram, React Docs). It guarantees deterministic output, atomic CSS classes, and zero runtime. Supports React Server Components natively.
StyleX requires a Babel plugin and produces an .stylex.js file. The API is similar to styled-components but with deterministic class names and no runtime.

section.name: migration-strategy
section.title: Migration Strategy: Runtime → Zero-Runtime
section.p: For large codebases, migrate incrementally: (1) audit existing styled components and list them by file, (2) convert one component/page at a time using vanilla-extract recipes, (3) run typecheck + Storybook visual diff after every batch, (4) commit per-batch so reverts are surgical, (5) keep the human in the loop for theming decisions (CSS variables vs Panda theme() API) — those become load-bearing later.
For SSR style extraction, the agent should produce registry/document changes and a Playwright assertion that no FOUC occurs on first paint.
Recommended subagents: faion-feature-executor (sequential per-component migration with a "build + visual-test" quality gate). faion-sdd-executor-agent (full SDD loop for large migrations).

Prompt pattern: "Convert src/components/Button.tsx from styled-components to vanilla-extract recipes. Preserve prop API. Output Button.css.ts and updated Button.tsx. Then write a story that exercises every variant." OR "Find all styled.X calls inside render bodies in src/. Return a refactor plan that lifts them to module scope and threads dynamic values via transient props or CSS variables."

section.name: best-practices
section.title: Best Practices
section.p: Pick exactly one CSS-in-JS library per app. Mixing styled-components + Emotion doubles runtime and breaks themes. Consolidate on vanilla-extract, Panda, or StyleX for new projects.
Keep *.css.ts files dependency-free of app code — only import other *.css.ts, types, and constants. Anything else slows the build sandbox.
Use CSS variables for any value that changes per render (theme, animation target). Avoid prop-driven styled.div for hot paths — set the variable via inline style instead.
For SSR with styled-components, always render through the registry and write a Playwright test that asserts style data-styled appears in the initial HTML.
Treat Sprinkles properties like a public API: every added property compiles to N classnames. Audit the generated CSS size in CI. Keep value arrays small to prevent CSS explosion.
Lift all styled components and recipe() calls to module scope. Dynamic styles should use CSS variables or transient props, never prop-conditional template strings.

section.name: cli-tools
section.title: CLI Tools and Setup
section.p: vanilla-extract: pnpm add @vanilla-extract/css, @vanilla-extract/recipes, @vanilla-extract/sprinkles. Build plugin: pnpm add -D @vanilla-extract/vite-plugin (or webpack/next plugin). Panda CSS: pnpm add @pandacss/dev && panda init. Meta StyleX: pnpm add @stylexjs/stylex. styled-components v6+: pnpm add styled-components. Emotion: pnpm add @emotion/react @emotion/styled. Emotion ESLint: npx @emotion/eslint-plugin.

section.name: services-and-tools
section.title: Services and Analysis Tools
section.p: Chromatic (SaaS): catches visual regressions during migration; treat as oracle for "did the styles actually change?". Bundlephobia (web): use to compare runtime cost; no API for agents — wrap with npm-bundle-size. Statoscope / Webpack Bundle Analyzer (OSS): agents can parse JSON output to verify CSS extraction worked. Linaria (OSS): alternative zero-runtime; tagged-template syntax is closer to styled-components for migration.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.title: AI Agent Gotchas and Failure Modes
section.p: Agents copy RecipeVariants<typeof button> but forget the matching defaultVariants; output throws at runtime because the recipe receives undefined.
LLMs often write vars: < color: '#3b82f6' > instead of using createVar() + assignVars, breaking theme switching.
For Emotion in App Router, generated code omits 'use client' and the CacheProvider; FOUC + hydration mismatch.
Agents reach for && css`...` template-string composition and lose TS types — prefer recipe variants over prop-conditional template strings.
When asked to "make it faster", agents memoize the wrong layer (memoizing css calls instead of lifting styled components out of render).
styled-components v6 + Next.js App Router needs styled-components/registry and compiler.styledComponents = true; agents routinely produce hydration-mismatch warnings without it.
vanilla-extract's .css.ts files run in a Node sandbox at build time — process.env, window, and dynamic imports break the build cryptically.
Sprinkles bundles every variant combo as static classnames; over-broad properties arrays explode CSS size by 5-10x.
Emotion css prop with TS strict mode requires /** @jsxImportSource @emotion/react */ per file or it silently degrades to plain style.

section.name: references
section.title: References and Documentation
section.p: vanilla-extract: https://vanilla-extract.style/ — comprehensive docs and examples. Panda CSS: https://panda-css.com — token system and recipe documentation. StyleX: https://stylexjs.com — Meta's deterministic CSS-in-JS. styled-components SSR: https://styled-components.com/docs/advanced#server-side-rendering — server-side rendering setup. Emotion SSR: https://emotion.sh/docs/ssr — Emotion server rendering. Josh Comeau, "The Styled-Components Happy Path": https://www.joshwcomeau.com/css/styled-components/ — best practices and patterns.

template.purpose: Complete vanilla-extract recipe with color and size variants
Bash script to flag styled.X definitions inside React component render bodies
